mnemonics.title: SECURE MOBILE
mnemonics.content: S-Secure your device with encryption, E-Enforce strong passwords, C-Check app permissions regularly, U-Update software always, R-Remote wipe enabled, E-Enable VPN on public Wi-Fi, M-Monitor Bluetooth/NFC, O-Obtain apps from official stores, B-Backup data frequently, I-Install security software, L-Lock SIM card, E-Educate yourself on phishing

mnemonics.title: MOBILE THREATS
mnemonics.content: M-Malware, O-Online phishing, B-Bluebugging/Bluetooth attacks, I-Identity theft, L-Loss/theft of device, E-Exploitation of vulnerabilities, - (dash for break), T-Trojans, H-Hooking (man-in-the-middle), R-Ransomware, E-Eavesdropping, A-Adware/Spyware, T-Travel virus (infecting via charging stations)

mnemonics.title: WIRELESS ENCRYPTION HIERARCHY
mnemonics.content: W-WEP (Worst Encryption - Easily Broken), P-WPA (Protection Arrived), A-WPA2 (Advanced Security), W-WPA3 (Wi-Fi Protected Access 3 - Most Secure)

mnemonics.title: AUTHENTICATION FACTORS
mnemonics.content: SOM - Something You Know (Password/PIN), Have (Token/SIM), Are (Biometric - Fingerprint/Face)

mnemonics.title: MDM FEATURES
mnemonics.content: C-Configuration management, A-App management, S-Security policy enforcement, E-Encrypted storage, W-Wipe (remote), A-Auditing/monitoring, R-Resource access control

acronyms.usage: Use when discussing centralized management and security of corporate mobile devices
Use when referring to broader mobility management including apps, content, and devices
Use when discussing employees using personal devices for work purposes
Use when referring to the latest and most secure wireless encryption standard
Use when referring to the name of a wireless network
Use when discussing layered security requiring multiple verification methods
Use when discussing short-range wireless communication for payments/data transfer
Use when discussing secure encrypted connection for public Wi-Fi safety
Use when discussing SIM card security and cloning threats
Use when discussing USB connections between mobile devices and external hardware

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a ladder: WEP at the bottom (weak wooden rung), WPA in middle (sturdy rung), WPA2 as strong steel rung, WPA3 at top (diamond-strength rung). Climb higher for better security.
Picture a central control tower (MDM server) with multiple airplane icons (mobile devices) on a screen. The tower can lock, wipe, or configure each device remotely like air traffic control.

visualTips.concept: Data at Rest vs Data in Transit
visualTips.visualization: Imagine a treasure chest (device storage) for data at rest - it's locked in your house. Data in transit is like treasure being moved in an armored truck on a highway - needs extra protection (encryption) during movement.

Visualize your personal phone as a pet dog that you bring to office. The dog (personal device) might bring outside germs (malware) or escape (data leak) if not properly trained (managed) and fenced (sandboxed).

Think of malware as a virus spreading through a city via multiple channels: infected apps (sneakers), malicious websites (subway), infected charging stations (water supply), and Bluetooth/nearby connections (airborne).
Picture a high-security bank vault with multiple locks: keycard (something you have), PIN (something you know), and face/fingerprint scanner (who you are). All three needed for maximum security.

analogies.analogy: Like putting your important documents in a safe with a combination lock - even if someone steals the safe, they cannot read your documents without the code.
Like having a self-destruct button on a secret agent's briefcase - if the device is lost/stolen, you can remotely destroy all sensitive information to prevent it falling into wrong hands.
Like each app living in its own isolated room in a hotel - apps cannot enter each other's rooms or access the hotel's master systems without special permissions.
Like sending a postcard through the mail - anyone along the route can read what you wrote. With VPN, it's like putting that postcard in a sealed, encrypted envelope only the recipient can open.
Like a school principal (IT admin) having master keys to all lockers (devices) - can check, lock, or clear any locker remotely but students (employees) still have personal use within rules.
Like removing the walls of a secured building to customize it - you gain freedom to modify but also remove all security systems, making it vulnerable to any intruder.
Like someone making an exact duplicate of your house keys - they can now enter and impersonate you, accessing all your services and incurring charges in your name.
Like needing both your password (brain) and your physical key (phone) to open a lock - even if someone learns your password, they cannot enter without your device.
